channel.title: Falls and Fractures on VitaminDWiki
channel.description: Recent content in Falls and Fractures on VitaminDWiki

item.title: 100,000 IU of Vitamin D monthly (27 ng) is not enough to reduce fractures and falls – meta-analysis
item.description:
• Effects of intermittent overload doses of oral vitamin D3 on serum 25(OH)D concentrations and the incidence rates of fractures, falls, and mortality in elderly individuals: A systematic review and meta- analysis

Effects of intermittent overload doses of oral vitamin D3 on serum 25(OH)D concentrations and the incidence rates of fractures, falls, and mortality in elderly individuals: A systematic review and meta- analysis

Download the PDF from VitaminDWiki

item.title: Vitamin D deficiency in children: 9%, but up to 75% in children with fractures
item.description:
• Vitamin D and Pediatric Bone Health – Important Information and Considerations for the Pediatric Orthopaedic Surgeon
      • You can test your child for extreme Vitamin D deficiency in 20 seconds

Vitamin D and Pediatric Bone Health – Important Information and Considerations for the Pediatric Orthopaedic Surgeon

Journal of the Pediatric Orthopaedic Society of North America https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jposna.2024.100042

Fatima Bouftas a, Clarabelle DeVries b

Highlights

  • Vitamin D plays a vital role in musculoskeletal health and optimizing vitamin D levels in childhood and adolescence is crucial to proper bone development.

item.title: Hip joint destroyed in months (rare) is associated with especially low Vitamin D
item.description:
• Vitamin D Deficiency Is Frequent in Patients with Rapidly Destructive Osteoarthritis—Data from a Single-Center Analysis

Vitamin D Deficiency Is Frequent in Patients with Rapidly Destructive Osteoarthritis—Data from a Single-Center Analysis

J. Clin. Med. 2024, 13(5), 1296; https://doi.org/10.3390/jcm13051296

Konstantin Horas 1,*,†ORCID,Gerrit Maier 2,†,Maximilian Rudert 1ORCID,Axel Jakuscheit 1ORCID,Manuel Weißenberger 1,3ORCID,Ioannis Stratos 1ORCID,Tizian Heinz 1,Dominik Rak 1ORCID,Philip Mark Anderson 1ORCID andJörg Arnholdt 4

Background: Rapidly destructive osteoarthritis (RDO) of the hip joint is characterised by the rapid destruction of the femoral head with or without acetabular involvement. There has been increasing interest in this disease over the past years; however, the entity is still poorly understood, and its pathophysiology remains unknown. Yet, there is ample evidence today that increased bone metabolism might play a role in the onset and progression of the disease. Vitamin D is of utmost importance to maintain a balanced bone metabolism. However, whether vitamin D deficiency is involved in disease development remains to be elucidated. Further, the vitamin D status of patients with RDO has not yet been analysed. For this reason, the objective of this study was to assess the vitamin D status of patients with RDO. Moreover, the aim was to clarify whether there is a difference in the vitamin D status of patients with RDO compared with patients with primary osteoarthritis (OA).

item.title: Vertigo incidence reduced 40% by just 7,000 IU Vitamin D given weekly - RCT
item.description:
• Vitamin D supplementation in preventing the recurrence of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo

Vitamin D supplementation in preventing the recurrence of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo

Tae Hoon Kong MD, PhD, Su Young Jung MD, Young Joon Seo MD, PhD, Dae Bo Shim MD, PhD

Investigative Otorhinolaryngology February 2024 https://doi.org/10.1002/lio2.1225

Objective

To evaluate the effect of vitamin D supplementation on the recurrence rate of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV).

Methods

A single-center, prospective, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group randomized controlled trial was conducted between November 2018 and May 2020. After successful treatment with canalith repositioning maneuvers, patients diagnosed with BPPV were randomized to either the vitamin D (n = 20) or placebo (n = 18) group. Only patients with serum vitamin D levels <20 ng mL−1 were included. The vitamin D group received 7000 IU of vitamin D weekly for a year, while the placebo group received a matching placebo drug. The final endpoint was the BPPV recurrence rate and correlation with serum vitamin D levels after 6 and 12 months in both groups.

item.title: Hip fractures 60% more likely in people who drank a lot of milk - Greger
item.description:
• Why Do Milk Drinkers Live Shorter Lives on Average?
      • VitaminDWiki - Calcium - Kale has 5X Calcium than skim milk, and is more bioavailable - Jan 2024

Why Do Milk Drinkers Live Shorter Lives on Average?

Dr. Greger

In my video on milk and bones, I discussed this set of studies, following 100,000 people for up to two decades, finding a 60 percent higher risk of hip fracture among women who drank a lot of milk. The researchers suggested it might be due to the galactose, which is a breakdown product of the milk sugar lactose, based on the fact that people with high levels in their blood––because they were born with an inability to detoxify the stuff––can end up with weakened bones. But that’s not all galactose can do.

item.title: Hip fracture risk was reduced by 4 times when Vitamin D was added to SERMS
item.description:
• Study: Selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs) with vitamin D composite agent can prevent fracture better than SERMs treatment:

Study: Selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs) with vitamin D composite agent can prevent fracture better than SERMs treatment:

based on the National Health Claims Database 2017-2019

Osteoporos Int 2024 Jan 19. doi: 10.1007/s00198-024-07022-7 Can be viewed at DeepDyve 1 month free trial

Seong-Eun Byun 1, Hasung Kim 2, Seung Yun Lee 2, Sang-Min Kim 3

item.title: Hip fractures are predicted by 10 factors – low Vitamin D is the biggest
item.description:

      • See also in VitaminDWiki

item.title: Higher air pollution associated with more fractures and lower levels of vitamin D
item.description:
• Exposure to multiple air pollutants and the risk of fractures: a large prospective population-based study

Exposure to multiple air pollutants and the risk of fractures: a large prospective population-based study

J Bone Miner Res. 2023 Jun 21. doi: 10.1002/jbmr.4872

Wenhao Qi # 1 2, Zhendong Mei # 1 2, Zhonghan Sun 1 2, Chenhao Lin 1 2, Jinran Lin 1 2, Jialin Li 1, John S Ji # 3, Yan Zheng 4 1 2

item.title: Bariatric surgery increases risk of bone fracture (less vitamin D adsorbed)
item.description:
Does weight loss surgery up the risk for bone fractures?

MDEdge

  • “The relative risk for fracture after RYGB and BPD-DS is reported to be 1.2-2.3 (that is, 20%-130% more than normal), whereas fracture risk after sleeve gastrectomy is still under study with some conflicting results. Fracture risk starts to increase 2-3 years after surgery and peaks at 5-plus years after surgery.”

  • “Weight loss surgery is currently recommended for people who have a body mass index greater than or equal to 35 regardless of obesity-related complication and may be considered for those with a BMI greater than or equal to 30”

item.title: Bone fractures of children not reduced if given little vitamin D, no Calcium etc. – RCT
item.description:
Gave 14,000 IU vitamin D weekly – independent of body weight (age 6-13)

Average vitamin D level achieved was only 30 ng after 3 years (in summer?)

Fracture prevention also needs Magnesium, Vitamin K2, and Omega-3

      • Influence of vitamin D supplementation on fracture risk, bone mineral density and bone biochemistry in Mongolian schoolchildren: multicenter double-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial
      • See also in VitaminDWiki

Influence of vitamin D supplementation on fracture risk, bone mineral density and bone biochemistry in Mongolian schoolchildren: multicenter double-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial

doi: 10.1101/2023.05.18.23290181. Preprint

item.title: Falls and frailty associated with low levels of both vitamins B12 and D3
item.description:
• Association of vitamins B12 and D3 with Balance and Falls in a sample of Greek older people
      • VitaminDWiki – Frailty and Vitamin D - many studies 15+

Association of vitamins B12 and D3 with Balance and Falls in a sample of Greek older people

J Musculoskelet Neuronal Interact . 2023 Jun 1;23(2):205-214.

Konstantinos Stolakis 1, Panagiotis Megas 2, Elias Panagiotopoulos 3, Manolis Mentis 4, Eleftheria Antoniadou 5, Xristina Kalivioti 5, Minos Tyllianakis 2, Zinon Kokkalis 2

item.title: Parkinson’s – Vitamin D, bone and falls
item.description:
• The Relationship Among Bone Mineral Density, Bone Biomarkers and Vitamin D Levels in Patients with Parkinson’s Disease.

The Relationship Among Bone Mineral Density, Bone Biomarkers and Vitamin D Levels in Patients with Parkinson’s Disease.

Clinical Laboratory, 01 Aug 2020, 66(8) DOI: 10.7754/clin.lab.2019.190615 PDF is behind a paywall

Can NU, Alagöz AN

Idiopathic Parkinson’s disease (IPD) is the most common age-related neurodegenerative movement disorder. It causes a decrease and deceleration in movements. It may also lead to loss of bone mineral density, vitamin D deficiency, falls and fractures due to various factors. As indicated in our study, determining the prevalence of osteopenia and osteoporosis in IPD is important to determine complications.

item.title: Fracture treatment 2000 dollars less expensive if also use Vitamin D and Calcium
item.description:
• Economic Burden of Osteoporotic Patients with Fracture: Effect of Treatment With or Without Calcium/Vitamin D Supplements
      • Vitamin D also prevents fractures
      • Vitamin D reduces the risk of death after hip fracture by 4X

Economic Burden of Osteoporotic Patients with Fracture: Effect of Treatment With or Without Calcium/Vitamin D Supplements

Nutrition and Dietary Supplements Pages 21-30 https://doi.org/10.2147/NDS.S234911

Luca Degli Esposti,Stefania Saragoni,Valentina Perrone,Stefania Sella,Margherita Andretta,Maurizio Rossini…

Background:

Fractures represent the most important complication of osteoporosis, in terms of loss of independency, chronic pain, increased risk of mortality, but also high healthcare costs.

item.title: Fractures 1.5 X more-likely if low serum Magnesium – meta-analysis
item.description:
• Association between Serum Magnesium and Fractures: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Observational Studies

Association between Serum Magnesium and Fractures: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Observational Studies

Nutrients 2023, 15(6), 1304; https://doi.org/10.3390/nu15061304

by Ligia J. Dominguez 1,2,*,Nicola Veronese 1ORCID,Stefano Ciriminna 1,José Luis Pérez-Albela 3,Vania Flora Vásquez-López 3,Santiago Rodas-Regalado 3,Giovanna Di Bella 1,Angela Parisi 1,Federica Tagliaferri 1 andMario Barbagallo 1ORCID

1 Geriatric Unit, Department of Internal Medicine and Geriatrics, University of Palermo, 90133 Palermo, Italy

item.title: Bones not helped by tiny Vitamin D and Mg, for a short time with no exercise
item.description:
• Combined vitamin D and magnesium supplementation does not influence markers of bone turnover or glycemic control: A randomized controlled clinical trial

Combined vitamin D and magnesium supplementation does not influence markers of bone turnover or glycemic control: A randomized controlled clinical trial

Nutr Res . 2023 Feb;110:33-43. doi: 10.1016/j.nutres.2022.12.005

Rosemary D Dall 1, May M Cheung 2, Patricia A Shewokis 1, Asma Altasan 1, Stella L Volpe 3, Renee Amori 4, Harpreet Singh 5, Deeptha Sukumar 6

item.title: Low-energy fractures in children with low vitamin D - many studies
item.description:
• 4.6 X more-likely for children to have low energy fracture if low Vitamin D - Feb 2023
      • Total fractures in children NOT associated with low vitamin D - meta-analysis June 2021
      • Children 60% less likely to have bone fractures if daily 2,800 IU of Vitamin D while pregnant - RCT followup Jan 2022
      • Higher risk of child forearm fracture if low vitamin D and perhaps low magnesium - 2022
      • Low and high energy fractures in Children- May 2022

4.6 X more-likely for children to have low energy fracture if low Vitamin D - Feb 2023

Vitamin D level and low-energy fracture risk in children and adolescents: a population-based case-control study of 45 cases
